1	---
2	title: Font Optimization
3	description: Learn how to optimize fonts in Next.js
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: See the API Reference for the full feature set of Next.js Font
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/components/font
9	---
10	
11	The [`next/font`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/font) module automatically optimizes your fonts and removes external network requests for improved privacy and performance.
12	
13	It includes **built-in self-hosting** for any font file. This means you can optimally load web fonts with no layout shift.
14	
15	<AppOnly>
16	
17	To start using `next/font`, import it from [`next/font/local`](#local-fonts) or [`next/font/google`](#google-fonts), call it as a function with the appropriate options, and set the `className` of the element you want to apply the font to. For example:
18	
19	```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
20	import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
21	
22	const geist = Geist({
23	  subsets: ['latin'],
24	})
25	
26	export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
27	  return (
28	    <html lang="en" className={geist.className}>
29	      <body>{children}</body>
30	    </html>
31	  )
32	}
33	```
34	
35	```jsx filename="app/layout.js" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
36	import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
37	
38	const geist = Geist({
39	  subsets: ['latin'],
40	})
41	
42	export default function Layout({ children }) {
43	  return (
44	    <html className={geist.className}>
45	      <body>{children}</body>
46	    </html>
47	  )
48	}
49	```
50	
51	Fonts are scoped to the component they're used in. To apply a font to your entire application, add it to the [Root Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout).
52	
53	</AppOnly>
54	
55	<PagesOnly>
56	
57	To start using `next/font`, import it from [`next/font/local`](#local-fonts) or [`next/font/google`](#google-fonts), call it as a function with the appropriate options, and set the `className` of the element you want to apply the font to. For example, you can apply fonts globally in your [Custom App](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-app) (`pages/_app`):
58	
59	```tsx filename="pages/_app.tsx" highlight={1,4-6,10} switcher
60	import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
61	import type { AppProps } from 'next/app'
62	
63	const geist = Geist({
64	  subsets: ['latin'],
65	})
66	
67	export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
68	  return (
69	    <main className={geist.className}>
70	      <Component {...pageProps} />
71	    </main>
72	  )
73	}
74	```
75	
76	```jsx filename="pages/_app.js" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
77	import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
78	
79	const geist = Geist({
80	  subsets: ['latin'],
81	})
82	
83	export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
84	  return (
85	    <main className={geist.className}>
86	      <Component {...pageProps} />
87	    </main>
88	  )
89	}
90	```
91	
92	</PagesOnly>
93	
94	## Google fonts
95	
96	You can automatically self-host any Google Font. Fonts are included stored as static assets and served from the same domain as your deployment, meaning no requests are sent to Google by the browser when the user visits your site.
97	
98	To start using a Google Font, import your chosen font from `next/font/google`:
99	
100	<AppOnly>
101	
102	```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher
103	import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
104	
105	const geist = Geist({
106	  subsets: ['latin'],
107	})
108	
109	export default function RootLayout({
110	  children,
111	}: {
112	  children: React.ReactNode
113	}) {
114	  return (
115	    <html lang="en" className={geist.className}>
116	      <body>{children}</body>
117	    </html>
118	  )
119	}
120	```
121

260	## Local fonts
261	
262	<AppOnly>
263	
264	To use a local font, import the `localFont` function from `next/font/local` and specify the [`src`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/font#src) of your local font file. The path is resolved relative to the file where `localFont` is called. Fonts can be stored anywhere in the project, including the [`public`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/public-folder) folder or co-located inside the `app` folder. For example, to use a font stored in `app/fonts/`:
265	
266	```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher
267	import localFont from 'next/font/local'
268	
269	const myFont = localFont({
270	  src: './my-font.woff2',
271	})
272	
273	export default function RootLayout({
274	  children,
275	}: {
276	  children: React.ReactNode
277	}) {
278	  return (
279	    <html lang="en" className={myFont.className}>
280	      <body>{children}</body>
281	    </html>
282	  )
283	}
284	```

Fonts can be stored in the [`public`](/docs/pages/api-reference/file-conventions/public-folder) folder or inside the `pages` folder. For example:

1 --- 2 title: Image Optimization 3 description: Learn how to optimize images in Next.js 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: See the API Reference for the full feature set of Next.js Image. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/components/image 9 --- 10 11 The Next.js [``](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image) component extends the HTML `` element to provide: 12 13 - **Size optimization:** Automatically serving correctly sized images for each device, using modern image formats like WebP. 14 - **Visual stability:** Preventing [layout shift](https://web.dev/articles/cls) automatically when images are loading. 15 - **Faster page loads:** Only loading images when they enter the viewport using native browser lazy loading, with optional blur-up placeholders. 16 - **Asset flexibility:** Resizing images on-demand, even images stored on remote servers. 17 18 To start using ``, import it from `next/image` and render it within your component. 19 20 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 21 import Image from 'next/image' 22 23 export default function Page() { 24 return 25 } 26 ``` 27 28 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 29 import Image from 'next/image' 30 31 export default function Page() { 32 return 33 } 34 ``` 35 36 The `src` property can be a [local](#local-images) or [remote](#remote-images) image. 37 38 > **🎥 Watch:** Learn more about how to use `next/image` → [YouTube (9 minutes)](https://youtu.be/IU_qq_c_lKA). 39 40 ## Local images 41 42 You can store static files, like images and fonts, under a folder called [`public`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/public-folder) in the root directory. Files inside `public` can then be referenced by your code starting from the base URL (`/`). 43 44 51 52 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 53 import Image from 'next/image' 54 55 export default function Page() { 56 return ( 57 63 ) 64 } 65 ``` 66 67 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 68 import Image from 'next/image' 69 70 export default function Page() { 71 return ( 72 78 ) 79 } 80 ``` 81 82 If the image is statically imported, Next.js will automatically determine the intrinsic [`width`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#width-and-height) and [`height`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#width-and-height). These values are used to determine the image ratio and prevent [Cumulative Layout Shift](https://web.dev/articles/cls) while your image is loading. 83 84 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 85 import Image from 'next/image' 86 import ProfileImage from './profile.png' 87 88 export default function Page() { 89 return ( 90 98 ) 99 } 100 ``` 101 102 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 103 import Image from 'next/image' 104 import ProfileImage from './profile.png' 105 106 export default function Page() { 107 return ( 108 116 ) 117 } 118 ``` 119 120 ### Images without static imports 121 122 If you can't use a static `import` for your images, you can use a dynamic `import()` in a Server Component to still get automatic `width`, `height`, and `blurDataURL`: 123 124 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 125 import Image from 'next/image' 126 127 async function PostImage({ 128 imageFilename, 129 alt, 130 }: { 131 imageFilename: string 132 alt: string 133 }) { 134 const { default: image } = await import( 135 `../content/blog/images/${imageFilename}` 136 ) 137 // image contains width, height, and blurDataURL 138 return 139 } 140 ``` 141 142 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 143 import Image from 'next/image' 144 145 async function PostImage({ imageFilename, alt }) { 146 const { default: image } = await import( 147 `../content/blog/images/${imageFilename}` 148 ) 149 // image contains width, height, and blurDataURL 150 return 151 } 152 ``` 153 154 If you have a [path alias](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#paths) configured (e.g. `@/`), you can use it instead of a relative path: 155 156 ```tsx 157 const { default: image } = await import( 158 `@/content/blog/images/${imageFilename}` 159 ) 160 ``` 161 162 The path must include a static prefix (like `../content/blog/images/`). Be as specific as possible, since **all** files matching that prefix are bundled. Only files in your specified directory are included, so external input cannot reach outside of it. 163 164 ## Remote images 165 166 To use a remote image, you can provide a URL string for the `src` property. 167 168 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 169 import Image from 'next/image' 170 171 export default function Page() { 172 return ( 173 179 ) 180 } 181 ``` 182 183 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 184 import Image from 'next/image' 185 186 export default function Page() { 187 return ( 188 194 ) 195 } 196 ``` 197 198 Since Next.js does not have access to remote files during the build process, you'll need to provide the [`width`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#width-and-height), [`height`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#width-and-height) and optional [`blurDataURL`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#blurdataurl) props manually. The `width` and `height` are used to infer the correct aspect ratio of image and avoid layout shift from the image loading in. Alternatively, you can use the [`fill` property](/docs/app/api-reference/components/image#fill) to make the image fill the size of the parent element. 199 200 To safely allow images from remote servers, you need to define a list of supported URL patterns in [`next.config.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js). Be as specific as possible to prevent malicious usage. For example, the following configuration will only allow images from a specific AWS S3 bucket: 201 202 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 203 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 204 205 const config: NextConfig = { 206 images: { 207 remotePatterns: [ 208 { 209 protocol: 'https', 210 hostname: 's3.amazonaws.com', 211 port: '', 212 pathname: '/my-bucket/**', 213 search: '', 214 }, 215 ], 216 }, 217 } 218 219 export default config 220 ``` 221 222 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 223 module.exports = { 224 images: { 225 remotePatterns: [ 226 { 227 protocol: 'https', 228 hostname: 's3.amazonaws.com', 229 port: '', 230 pathname: '/my-bucket/**', 231 search: '', 232 }, 233 ], 234 }, 235 } 236 ``` 237

60 ``` 61 62 Import the CSS file in your root layout: 63 64 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 65 import './globals.css' 66 67 export default function RootLayout({ 68 children, 69 }: { 70 children: React.ReactNode 71 }) { 72 return ( 73 74 {children} 75 76 ) 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 81 import './globals.css' 82 83 export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 84 return ( 85 86 {children} 87 88 ) 89 } 90 ``` 91 92 Now you can start using Tailwind's utility classes in your application: 93 94 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 95 export default function Page() { 96 return ( 97

This allows you to use the same class in different files without worrying about naming collisions. 191 192 193 194 To start using CSS Modules, create a new file with the extension `.module.css` and import it into any component inside the `app` directory: 195 196 ```css filename="app/blog/blog.module.css" 197 .blog { 198 padding: 24px; 199 } 200 ``` 201 202 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 203 import styles from './blog.module.css' 204 205 export default function Page() { 206 return
207 } 208 ``` 209 210 ```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 211 import styles from './blog.module.css' 212 213 export default function Page() { 214 return
215 } 216 ``` 217 218 219 220 221 222 To start using CSS Modules, create a new file with the extension `.module.css` and import it into any component inside the `pages` directory: 223 224 ```css filename="/styles/blog.module.css" 225 .blog { 226 padding: 24px; 227 } 228 ``` 229 230 ```tsx filename="pages/blog/index.tsx" switcher 231 import styles from './blog.module.css' 232 233 export default function Page() { 234 return
235 } 236 ``` 237 238 ```jsx filename="pages/blog/index.js" switcher 239 import styles from './blog.module.css' 240 241 export default function Page() { 242 return
243 } 244 ``` 245 246 247 248 ## Global CSS 249 250 You can use global CSS to apply styles across your application. 251 252 253 254 Create a `app/global.css` file and import it in the root layout to apply the styles to **every route** in your application: 255 256 ```css filename="app/global.css" 257 body { 258 padding: 20px 20px 60px; 259 max-width: 680px; 260 margin: 0 auto; 261 } 262 ``` 263 264 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 265 // These styles apply to every route in the application 266 import './global.css' 267 268 export default function RootLayout({ 269 children, 270 }: { 271 children: React.ReactNode 272 }) { 273 return ( 274 275 {children} 276 277 ) 278 } 279 ``` 280 281 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 282 // These styles apply to every route in the application 283 import './global.css' 284 285 export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 286 return ( 287 288 {children} 289 290 ) 291 } 292 ``` 293 294 > **Good to know:** Global styles can be imported into any layout, page, or component inside the `app` directory. However, since Next.js uses React's built-in support for stylesheets to integrate with Suspense, this currently does not remove stylesheets as you navigate between routes which can lead to conflicts. We recommend using global styles for _truly_ global CSS (like Tailwind's base styles), [Tailwind CSS](#tailwind-css) for component styling, and [CSS Modules](#css-modules) for custom scoped CSS when needed. 295 296 297 298 299 300 Import the stylesheet in the `pages/_app.js` file to apply the styles to **every route** in your application: 301 302 ```tsx filename="pages/_app.js" 303 import '@/styles/global.css' 304 305 export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) { 306 return 307 } 308 ``` 309 310 Due to the global nature of stylesheets, and to avoid conflicts, you should import them inside [`pages/_app.js`](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-app). 311 312 313 314 ## External stylesheets 315 316 317 318 Stylesheets published by external packages can be imported anywhere in the `app` directory, including colocated components: 319 320 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 321 import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.css' 322 323 export default function RootLayout({ 324 children, 325 }: { 326 children: React.ReactNode 327 }) { 328 return ( 329 330 {children} 331 332 ) 333 } 334 ``` 335 336 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 337 import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.css' 338 339 export default function RootLayout({ children }) { 340 return ( 341 342 {children} 343 344 ) 345 } 346 ``` 347 348 > **Good to know:** In React 19, `` can also be used. See the [React `link` documentation](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/components/link) for more information. 349 350 351 352 353 354 Next.js allows you to import CSS files from a JavaScript file. This is possible because Next.js extends the concept of [`import`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Statements/import) beyond JavaScript. 355 356 ### Import styles from `node_modules` 357 358 Since Next.js **9.5.4**, importing a CSS file from `node_modules` is permitted anywhere in your application. 359 360 For global stylesheets, like `bootstrap` or `nprogress`, you should import the file inside `pages/_app.js`. For example: 361 362 ```jsx filename="pages/_app.js" 363 import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.css' 364 365 export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) { 366 return 367 } 368 ``` 369 370 To import CSS required by a third-party component, you can do so in your component. For example: 371 372 ```jsx filename="components/example-dialog.js" 373 import { useState } from 'react' 374 import { Dialog } from '@reach/dialog' 375 import VisuallyHidden from '@reach/visually-hidden' 376 import '@reach/dialog/styles.css' 377 378 function ExampleDialog(props) { 379 const [showDialog, setShowDialog] = useState(false) 380 const open = () => setShowDialog(true) 381 const close = () => setShowDialog(false) 382 383 return ( 384

The **order of your CSS** depends on the **order you import styles in your code**. 403 404 For example, `base-button.module.css` will be ordered before `page.module.css` since `` is imported before `page.module.css`: 405 406 ```tsx filename="page.tsx" switcher 407 import { BaseButton } from './base-button' 408 import styles from './page.module.css' 409 410 export default function Page() { 411 return 412 } 413 ``` 414 415 ```jsx filename="page.js" switcher 416 import { BaseButton } from './base-button' 417 import styles from './page.module.css' 418 419 export default function Page() { 420 return 421 } 422 ``` 423 424 ```tsx filename="base-button.tsx" switcher 425 import styles from './base-button.module.css' 426 427 export function BaseButton() { 428 return

2	title: Upgrading
3	description: Learn how to upgrade your Next.js application to the latest version or canary.
4	related:
5	  title: Version guides
6	  description: See the version guides for in-depth upgrade instructions.
7	  links:
8	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-16
9	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-15
10	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-14
11	---
12	
13	## Latest version
14	
15	To update to the latest version of Next.js, you can use the `upgrade` command:
16	
17	```bash package="pnpm"
18	pnpm next upgrade
19	```
20	
21	```bash package="npm"
22	npx next upgrade
23	```
24	
25	```bash package="yarn"
26	yarn next upgrade
27	```
28	
29	```bash package="bun"
30	bunx next upgrade
31	```
32	
33	Versions before Next.js 16.1.0 do not support the `upgrade` command and need to use a separate package instead:
34	
35	```bash filename="Terminal"
36	npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
37	```
38	
39	If you prefer to upgrade manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:
40	
41	```bash package="pnpm"
42	pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
43	```
44	
45	```bash package="npm"
46	npm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
47	```
48	
49	```bash package="yarn"
50	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
51	```
52	
53	```bash package="bun"
54	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
55	```
56	
57	## Canary version
58	
59	To update to the latest canary, make sure you're on the latest version of Next.js and everything is working as expected. Then, run the following command:
60	
61	```bash package="pnpm"
62	pnpm add next@canary
63	```
64	
65	```bash package="npm"
66	npm i next@canary
67	```
68	
69	```bash package="yarn"
70	yarn add next@canary
71	```
72	
73	```bash package="bun"
74	bun add next@canary
75	```
76	
77	### Features available in canary
78	
79	The following features are currently available in canary:
80	
81	**Authentication**:
82	
83	- [`forbidden`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden)
84	- [`unauthorized`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unauthorized)
85	- [`forbidden.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/forbidden)
86	- [`unauthorized.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/unauthorized)
87	- [`authInterrupts`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/authInterrupts)

1 --- 2 title: Metadata and OG images 3 description: Learn how to add metadata to your pages and create dynamic OG images. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the Metadata APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata 9 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport 10 - app/api-reference/functions/image-response 11 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons 13 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image 14 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots 15 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap 16 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots 17 --- 18 19 The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include: 20 21 1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata) 22 2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata) 23 3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images). 24 25 With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools. 26 27 The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components. 28 29 ## Default fields 30 31 There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata: 32 33 - The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website. 34 - The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices. 35 36 ```html 37 38 39 ``` 40 41 The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)). 42 43 ## Static metadata 44 45 To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route: 46 47 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 48 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 49 50 export const metadata: Metadata = { 51 title: 'My Blog', 52 description: '...', 53 } 54 55 export default function Layout() {} 56 ``` 57 58 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 59 export const metadata = { 60 title: 'My Blog', 61 description: '...', 62 } 63 64 export default function Layout() {} 65 ``` 66 67 You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields). 68 69 ## Generated metadata 70 71 You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post: 72 73 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 74 import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next' 75 76 type Props = { 77 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 78 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 79 } 80 81 export async function generateMetadata( 82 { params, searchParams }: Props, 83 parent: ResolvingMetadata 84 ): Promise { 85 const slug = (await params).slug 86 87 // fetch post information 88 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 89 res.json() 90 ) 91 92 return { 93 title: post.title, 94 description: post.description, 95 } 96 } 97 98 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }: Props) {} 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 102 export async function generateMetadata({ params, searchParams }, parent) { 103 const slug = (await params).slug 104 105 // fetch post information 106 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 107 res.json() 108 ) 109 110 return { 111 title: post.title, 112 description: post.description, 113 } 114 } 115 116 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {} 117 ``` 118 119 ### Streaming metadata 120 121 For dynamically rendered pages, Next.js streams metadata separately, injecting it into the HTML once `generateMetadata` resolves, without blocking UI rendering. 122 123 Streaming metadata improves perceived performance by allowing visual content to stream first. 124 125 Streaming metadata is **disabled for bots and crawlers** that expect metadata to be in the `` tag (e.g. `Twitterbot`, `Slackbot`, `Bingbot`). These are detected by using the User Agent header from the incoming request. 126 127 You can customize or **disable** streaming metadata completely, with the [`htmlLimitedBots`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots#disabling) option in your Next.js config file. 128 129 Prerendered pages don’t use streaming since metadata is resolved at build time. 130 131 Learn more about [streaming metadata](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#streaming-metadata). 132 133 ### Memoizing data requests 134 135 There may be cases where you need to fetch the **same** data for metadata and the page itself. To avoid duplicate requests, you can use React's [`cache` function](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) to memoize the return value and only fetch the data once. For example, to fetch the blog post information for both the metadata and the page: 136 137 ```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" highlight={5} switcher 138 import { cache } from 'react' 139 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 140 141 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 142 export const getPost = cache(async (slug: string) => { 143 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 144 return res 145 }) 146 ``` 147 148 ```js filename="app/lib/data.js" highlight={5} switcher 149 import { cache } from 'react' 150 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 151 152 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 153 export const getPost = cache(async (slug) => { 154 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 155 return res 156 }) 157 ``` 158 159 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 160 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 161 162 export async function generateMetadata({ 163 params, 164 }: { 165 params: { slug: string } 166 }) { 167 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 168 return { 169 title: post.title, 170 description: post.description, 171 } 172 } 173 174 export default async function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { 175 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 176 return
{post.title}
177 } 178 ``` 179 180 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 181 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 182 183 export async function generateMetadata({ params }) { 184 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 185 return { 186 title: post.title, 187 description: post.description, 188 } 189 } 190 191 export default async function Page({ params }) { 192 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 193 return
{post.title}
194 } 195 ``` 196 197 ## File-based metadata 198 199 The following special files are available for metadata: 200 201 - [favicon.ico, apple-icon.jpg, and icon.jpg](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons) 202 - [opengraph-image.jpg and twitter-image.jpg](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) 203 - [robots.txt](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots) 204 - [sitemap.xml](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap) 205 206 You can use these for static metadata, or you can programmatically generate these files with code. 207 208 ## Favicons 209 210 Favicons are small icons that represent your site in bookmarks and search results. To add a favicon to your application, create a `favicon.ico` and add to the root of the app folder. 211 212 219 220 > You can also programmatically generate favicons using code. See the [favicon docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons) for more information. 221 222 ## Static Open Graph images 223 224 Open Graph (OG) images are images that represent your site in social media. To add a static OG image to your application, create a `opengraph-image.jpg` file in the root of the app folder. 225 226 233 234 You can also add OG images for specific routes by creating a `opengraph-image.jpg` deeper down the folder structure. For example, to create an OG image specific to the `/blog` route, add a `opengraph-image.jpg` file inside the `blog` folder. 235 236 243 244 The more specific image will take precedence over any OG images above it in the folder structure. 245 246 > Other image formats such as `jpeg`, `png`, and `gif` are also supported. See the [Open Graph Image docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) for more information. 247 248 ## Generated Open Graph images 249 250 The [`ImageResponse` constructor](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/image-response) allows you to generate dynamic images using JSX and CSS. This is useful for OG images that depend on data. 251 252 For example, to generate a unique OG image for each blog post, add a `opengraph-image.tsx` file inside the `blog` folder, and import the `ImageResponse` constructor from `next/og`: 253 254 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx" switcher 255 import { ImageResponse } from 'next/og' 256 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 257 258 // Image metadata 259 export const size = { 260 width: 1200, 261 height: 630, 262 } 263 264 export const contentType = 'image/png' 265 266 // Image generation 267 export default async function Image({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { 268 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 269 270 return new ImageResponse( 271 ( 272 // ImageResponse JSX element 273
284 {post.title} 285

content.content:
1	---
2	title: How to upgrade to version 16
3	nav_title: Version 16
4	description: Upgrade your Next.js Application from Version 15 to 16.
5	---
6	
7	{/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `<PagesOnly>Content</PagesOnly>` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */}
8	
9	## Upgrading from 15 to 16
10	
11	### Using AI Agents with Next.js DevTools MCP
12	
13	If you're using an AI coding assistant that supports the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io), you can use the **Next.js DevTools MCP** to automate the upgrade process and migration tasks.
14	
15	#### Setup
16	
17	Add the following configuration to your MCP client, for each coding agent you can read [this section](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp#mcp-client-configuration) for configuration details.
18	
19	**example:**
20	
21	```json filename=".mcp.json"
22	{
23	  "mcpServers": {
24	    "next-devtools": {
25	      "command": "npx",
26	      "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"]
27	    }
28	  }
29	}
30	```
31	
32	For more information, visit the [`next-devtools-mcp`](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp) documentation to configure with your MCP client.
33	
34	> **Note:** Using `next-devtools-mcp@latest` ensures that your MCP client will always use the latest version of the Next.js DevTools MCP server.
35	
36	#### Example Prompts
37	
38	Once configured, you can use natural language prompts to upgrade your Next.js app:
39	
40	**To upgrade to Next.js 16:**
41	
42	Connect to your coding agent and then prompt:
43	
44	```txt
45	Next Devtools, help me upgrade my Next.js app to version 16
46	```
47	
48	**To migrate to Cache Components (after upgrading to v16):**
49	
50	Connect to your coding agent and then prompt:
51	
52	```txt
53	Next Devtools, migrate my Next.js app to cache components
54	```
55	
56	Learn more in the documentation [here](/docs/app/guides/mcp).
57	
58	### Using the Codemod
59	
60	To update to Next.js version 16, you can use the `upgrade` [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160):
61	
62	```bash package="pnpm"
63	pnpm dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
64	```
65	
66	```bash package="npm"
67	npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
68	```
69	
70	```bash package="yarn"
71	yarn dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
72	```
73	
74	```bash package="bun"
75	bunx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
76	```
77	
78	The [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160) is able to:
79	
80	- Update `next.config.js` to use the new `turbopack` configuration
81	- Migrate from `next lint` to the ESLint CLI
82	- Migrate from deprecated `middleware` convention to `proxy`
83	- Remove `unstable_` prefix from stabilized APIs
84	- Remove `experimental_ppr` Route Segment Config from pages and layouts
85	
86	If you prefer to do it manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:
87	
88	```bash package="pnpm"
89	pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
90	```
91	
92	```bash package="npm"
93	npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
94	```
95	
96	```bash package="yarn"
97	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
98	```
99	
100	```bash package="bun"
101	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest
102	```
103	
104	If you are using TypeScript, ensure you also upgrade `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` to their latest versions.
105	
106	## Node.js runtime and browser support
107	
108	| Requirement   | Change / Details                                                   |
109	| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
110	| Node.js 20.9+ | Minimum version now `20.9.0` (LTS); Node.js 18 no longer supported |
111	| TypeScript 5+ | Minimum version now `5.1.0`                                        |
112	| Browsers      | Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+                 |
113	
114	## Turbopack by default
115	
116	Starting with **Next.js 16**, Turbopack is stable and used by default with `next dev` and `next build`
117	
118	Previously you had to enable Turbopack using `--turbopack`, or `--turbo`.
119	
120	```json filename="package.json"
121	{
122	  "scripts": {
123	    "dev": "next dev --turbopack",
124	    "build": "next build --turbopack",
125	    "start": "next start"
126	  }
127	}
128	```
129	
130	This is no longer necessary. You can update your `package.json` scripts:
131	
132	```json filename="package.json"
133	{
134	  "scripts": {
135	    "dev": "next dev",
136	    "build": "next build",
137	    "start": "next start"
138	  }
139	}
140	```
141	
142	If your project has a [custom `webpack`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack) configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will **fail** to prevent misconfiguration issues.
143	
144	You have a few different ways to address this:
145	
146	- **Use Turbopack anyway:** Run with `next build --turbopack` to build using Turbopack and ignore your `webpack` config.
147	- **Switch to Turbopack fully:** Migrate your `webpack` config to Turbopack-compatible options.
148	- **Keep using Webpack:** Use the `--webpack` flag to opt out of Turbopack and build with Webpack.
149	
150	> **Good to know**: If you see failing builds because a `webpack` configuration was found, but you don't define one yourself, it is likely that a plugin is adding a `webpack` option
151	
152	### Opting out of Turbopack
153	
154	If you need to continue using Webpack, you can opt out with the `--webpack` flag. For example, to use Turbopack in development but Webpack for production builds:
155	
156	```json filename="package.json"
157	{
158	  "scripts": {
159	    "dev": "next dev",
160	    "build": "next build --webpack",
161	    "start": "next start"
162	  }
163	}
164	```
165	
166	We recommend using Turbopack for development and production. Submit a comment to this [thread](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/77721), if you are unable to switch to Turbopack.
167	
168	### Turbopack configuration location
169	
170	The `experimental.turbopack` configuration is out of experimental.
171	
172	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
173	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
174	
175	// Next.js 15 - experimental.turbopack
176	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
177	  experimental: {
178	    turbopack: {
179	      // options
180	    },
181	  },
182	}
183	
184	export default nextConfig
185	```
186	
187	You can use it as a top-level `turbopack` option:
188	
189	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
190	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
191	
192	// Next.js 16 - turbopack at the top level of nextConfig
193	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
194	  turbopack: {
195	    // options
196	  },
197	}
198	
199	export default nextConfig
200	```

200 ``` 201 202 Make sure to review the `Turbopack` configuration [options](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack). **Next.js 16** introduces various improvements and new options, for example: 203 204 - [Advanced Webpack loader conditions](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#advanced-webpack-loader-conditions) 205 - [debugIds](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#debug-ids) 206 207 ### Resolve alias fallback 208 209 In some projects, client-side code may import files containing Node.js native modules. This will cause `Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'` type of errors. 210 211 When this happens, you should refactor your code so that your client-side bundles do not reference these Node.js native modules. 212 213 However, in some cases, this might not be possible. In Webpack the `resolve.fallback` option was typically used to **silence** the error. Turbopack offers a similar option, using `turbopack.resolveAlias`. In this case, tell Turbopack to load an empty module when `fs` is requested for the browser. 214 215 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 216 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 217 218 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 219 turbopack: { 220 resolveAlias: { 221 fs: { 222 browser: './empty.ts', // We recommend to fix code imports before using this method 223 }, 224 }, 225 }, 226 } 227 228 export default nextConfig 229 ``` 230 231 It is preferable to refactor your modules so that client code doesn't ever import from modules using Node.js native modules. 232 233 ### Sass node_modules imports 234 235 Turbopack fully supports importing Sass files from `node_modules`. Note that while Webpack allowed the legacy tilde (`~`) prefix, Turbopack does not support this syntax. 236 237 In Webpack: 238 239 ```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 240 @import '~bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 241 ``` 242 243 In Turbopack: 244 245 ```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 246 @import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 247 ``` 248 249 If changing the imports is not possible, you can use `turbopack.resolveAlias`. For example: 250 251 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 252 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 253 254 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 255 turbopack: { 256 resolveAlias: { 257 '~*': '*', 258 }, 259 }, 260 } 261 262 export default nextConfig 263 ``` 264 265 ### Turbopack File System Caching (beta) 266 267 Turbopack now supports filesystem caching in development, storing compiler artifacts on disk between runs for significantly faster compile times across restarts. 268 269 Enable filesystem caching in your configuration: 270 271 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 272 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 273 274 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 275 experimental: { 276 turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 277 }, 278 } 279 280 export default nextConfig 281 ``` 282 283 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 284 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 285 const nextConfig = { 286 experimental: { 287 turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 288 }, 289 } 290 291 module.exports = nextConfig 292 ``` 293 294 ## Async Request APIs (Breaking change) 295 296 Version 15 introduced [Async Request APIs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15#async-request-apis-breaking-change) as a breaking change, with **temporary** synchronous compatibility. 297 298 Starting with **Next.js 16**, synchronous access is fully removed. These APIs can only be accessed asynchronously. 299 300 - [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) 301 - [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) 302 - [`draftMode`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/draft-mode) 303 - `params` in [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [`route.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), [`default.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default), [`opengraph-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#opengraph-image), [`twitter-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#twitter-image), [`icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#icon), and [`apple-icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#apple-icon). 304 - `searchParams` in [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) 305 306 Use the [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#migrate-to-async-dynamic-apis) to migrate to async Request-time APIs. 307 308 ### Migrating types for async Request-time APIs 309 310 To help migrate to async `params` and `searchParams`, you can run [`npx next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options) to automatically generate these globally available types helpers: 311 312 - [`PageProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper) 313 - [`LayoutProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper) 314 - [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) 315 316 > **Good to know**: `typegen` was introduced in Next.js 15.5 317 318 This simplifies type-safe migration to the new async API pattern, and enables you to update your components with full type safety, for example: 319 320 ```tsx filename="/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 321 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 322 const { slug } = await props.params 323 const query = await props.searchParams 324 return

Blog Post: {slug}

325 } 326 ``` 327 328 This approach gives you fully type-safe access to `props.params`, including the `slug`, and to `searchParams`, directly within your page. 329 330 ## Async parameters for icon, and open-graph Image (Breaking change) 331 332 > The props passed to the image generating functions in `opengraph-image`, `twitter-image`, `icon`, and `apple-icon`, are now Promises. 333 334 In previous versions, both the `Image` (image generation function), and the `generateImageMetadata` received a `params` object. The `id` returned by `generateImageMetadata` was passed as a string to the image generation function. 335 336 ```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 337 // Next.js 15 - synchronous params access 338 export function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 339 const { slug } = params 340 return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 341 } 342 343 // Next.js 15 - synchronous params and id access 344 export default function Image({ params, id }) { 345 const slug = params.slug 346 const imageId = id // string 347 // ... 348 } 349 ``` 350 351 Starting with **Next.js 16**, to align with the [Async Request APIs](#async-request-apis-breaking-change) change, the image generating function now receives `params` and `id` as promises. The `generateImageMetadata` function continues to receive synchronous `params`. 352 353 ```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 354 export async function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 355 const { slug } = params 356 return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 357 } 358 359 // Next.js 16 - asynchronous params and id access 360 export default async function Image({ params, id }) { 361 const { slug } = await params // params now async 362 const imageId = await id // id is now Promise when using generateImageMetadata 363 // ... 364 } 365 ``` 366 367 ## Async `id` parameter for `sitemap` (Breaking change) 368 369 Previously, the `id` values returned from [`generateSitemaps`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-sitemaps) were passed directly to the `sitemap` generating function. 370 371 ```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 372 export async function generateSitemaps() { 373 return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 374 } 375 376 // Next.js 15 - synchronous id access 377 export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 378 const start = id * 50000 // id is a number 379 // ... 380 } 381 ``` 382 383 Starting with **Next.js 16**, the `sitemap` generating function now receives `id` as a promise. 384 385 ```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 386 export async function generateSitemaps() { 387 return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 388 } 389 390 // Next.js 16 - asynchronous id access 391 export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 392 const resolvedId = await id // id is now Promise 393 const start = Number(resolvedId) * 50000 394 // ... 395 } 396 ``` 397 398 ## React 19.2 399 400 The App Router in **Next.js 16** uses the latest React [Canary release](https://react.dev/blog/2023/05/03/react-canaries), which includes the newly released React 19.2 features and other features being incrementally stabilized. Highlights include: 401 402 - **[View Transitions](https://react.dev/reference/react/ViewTransition)**: Animate elements that update inside a Transition or navigation 403 - **[`useEffectEvent`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffectEvent)**: Extract non-reactive logic from Effects into reusable Effect Event functions 404 - **[Activity](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity)**: Render "background activity" by hiding UI with `display: none` while maintaining state and cleaning up Effects 405 406 Learn more in the [React 19.2 announcement](https://react.dev/blog/2025/10/01/react-19-2). 407 408 ## React Compiler Support 409 410 Built-in support for the React Compiler is now stable in **Next.js 16** following the React Compiler's 1.0 release. The React Compiler automatically memoizes components, reducing unnecessary re-renders with zero manual code changes. 411 412 The `reactCompiler` configuration option has been promoted from `experimental` to stable. It is not enabled by default as we continue gathering build performance data across different application types. 413 414 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 415 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 416 417 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 418 reactCompiler: true, 419 } 420 421 export default nextConfig 422 ``` 423 424 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 425 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 426 const nextConfig = { 427 reactCompiler: true, 428 } 429 430 module.exports = nextConfig 431 ``` 432 433 Install the latest version of the React Compiler plugin: 434 435 ```bash package="pnpm" 436 pnpm add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 437 ``` 438 439 ```bash package="npm" 440 npm install -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 441 ``` 442 443 ```bash package="yarn" 444 yarn add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 445 ``` 446 447 ```bash package="bun" 448 bun add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 449 ``` 450 451 > **Good to know:** Expect compile times in development and during builds to be higher when enabling this option as the React Compiler relies on Babel. 452 453 ## Caching APIs 454 455 ### revalidateTag 456 457 [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) now requires a second argument specifying a [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife#reference) profile. The single-argument form is deprecated and will produce a TypeScript error. 458 459 ```ts 460 // Before 461 revalidateTag('posts') 462 463 // After 464 revalidateTag('posts', 'max') 465 ``` 466 467 If you need immediate expiration rather than stale-while-revalidate, use [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) in Server Actions instead. 468 469 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 470 'use server' 471 472 import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 473 474 export async function updateArticle(articleId: string) { 475 // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 476 revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 477 } 478 ``` 479 480 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 481 'use server' 482 483 import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 484 485 export async function updateArticle(articleId) { 486 // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 487 revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 488 } 489 ``` 490 491 Use `revalidateTag` for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background. 492 493 ### updateTag 494 495 [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) is a new [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data#what-are-server-functions)-only API that provides **read-your-writes** semantics, where a user makes a change and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data. 496 497 It does this by expiring and immediately refreshing data within the same request. 498 499 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 500 'use server' 501 502 import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 503 504 export async function updateUserProfile(userId: string, profile: Profile) { 505 await db.users.update(userId, profile) 506 507 // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 508 updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 509 } 510 ``` 511 512 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 513 'use server' 514 515 import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 516 517 export async function updateUserProfile(userId, profile) { 518 await db.users.update(userId, profile) 519 520 // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 521 updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 522 } 523 ``` 524 525 This ensures interactive features reflect changes immediately. Perfect for forms, user settings, and any workflow where users expect to see their updates instantly. 526 527 Learn more about when to use `updateTag` or `revalidateTag` [here](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag#when-to-use-updatetag). 528 529 ### refresh 530 531 [`refresh`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/refresh) allows you to refresh the client router from within a Server Action. 532 533 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 534 'use server' 535 536 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 537 538 export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId: string) { 539 // Update the notification in the database 540 await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 541 542 // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 543 refresh() 544 } 545 ``` 546 547 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 548 'use server' 549 550 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 551 552 export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId) { 553 // Update the notification in the database 554 await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 555 556 // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 557 refresh() 558 } 559 ``` 560 561 Use it when you need to refresh the client router after performing an action. 562 563 ### cacheLife and cacheTag 564 565 [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) and [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) are now stable. The `unstable_` prefix is no longer needed. 566 567 Wherever you had aliased imports like: 568 569 ```ts 570 import { 571 unstable_cacheLife as cacheLife, 572 unstable_cacheTag as cacheTag, 573 } from 'next/cache' 574 ``` 575 576 You can update your imports to: 577 578 ```ts 579 import { cacheLife, cacheTag } from 'next/cache' 580 ``` 581 582 ## Enhanced Routing and Navigation 583 584 **Next.js 16** includes a complete overhaul of the routing and navigation system, making page transitions leaner and faster. This optimizes how Next.js prefetches and caches navigation data: 585 586 - **Layout deduplication**: When prefetching multiple URLs with a shared layout, the layout is downloaded once. 587 - **Incremental prefetching**: Next.js only prefetches parts not already in cache, rather than entire pages. 588 589 These changes require **no code modifications** and are designed to improve performance across all apps. 590 591 However, you may see more individual prefetch requests with much lower total transfer sizes. We believe this is the right trade-off for nearly all applications. 592 593 If the increased request count causes issues, please let us know by creating an [issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues) or [discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions) item. 594 595 ## Partial Prerendering (PPR) 596 597 **Next.js 16** removes the experimental **Partial Prerendering (PPR)** flag and configuration options, including the route level segment `experimental_ppr`. 598 599 Starting with **Next.js 16**, you can opt into PPR using the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) configuration.

21 22 --- 23 24 ## Documentation 25 26 For full documentation, visit [tailwindcss.com](https://tailwindcss.com). 27 28 ## Community 29 30 For help, discussion about best practices, or feature ideas: 31 32 [Discuss Tailwind CSS on GitHub](https://github.com/tailwindlabs/tailwindcss/discussions) 33 34 ## Contributing 35 36 If you're interested in contributing to Tailwind CSS, please read our [contributing docs](https://github.com/tailwindlabs/tailwindcss/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md) **before submitting a pull request**. 37 38 --- 39 40 ## `@tailwindcss/postcss` plugin API 41 42 ### Changing where the plugin searches for source files 43 44 You can use the `base` option (defaults to the current working directory) to change the directory in which the plugin searches for source files: 45 46 ```js 47 import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/postcss' 48 49 export default { 50 plugins: [ 51 tailwindcss({ 52 base: path.resolve(__dirname, './path'), 53 }), 54 ], 55 } 56 ``` 57 58 ### Enabling or disabling Lightning CSS 59 60 By default, this plugin detects whether or not the CSS is being built for production by checking the `NODE_ENV` environment variable. When building for production Lightning CSS will be enabled otherwise it is disabled. 61 62 If you want to always enable or disable Lightning CSS the `optimize` option may be used: 63 64 ```js 65 import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/postcss' 66 67 export default { 68 plugins: [ 69 tailwindcss({ 70 // Enable or disable Lightning CSS 71 optimize: false, 72 }), 73 ], 74 } 75 ``` 76 77 It's also possible to keep Lightning CSS enabled but disable minification: 78 79 ```js 80 import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/postcss' 81 82 export default { 83 plugins: [ 84 tailwindcss({ 85 optimize: { minify: false }, 86 }), 87 ], 88 } 89 ``` 90 91 ### Enabling or disabling `url(…)` rewriting 92 93 Our PostCSS plugin can rewrite `url(…)`s for you since it also handles `@import` (no `postcss-import` is needed). This feature is enabled by default. 94 95 In some situations the bundler or framework you're using may provide this feature itself. In this case you can set `transformAssetUrls` to `false` to disable this feature: 96 97 ```js 98 import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/postcss' 99 100 export default { 101 plugins: [ 102 tailwindcss({ 103 // Disable `url(…)` rewriting 104 transformAssetUrls: false, 105 106 // Enable `url(…)` rewriting (the default) 107 transformAssetUrls: true, 108 }), 109 ], 110 } 111 ``` 112 113 You may also pass options to `optimize` to enable Lighting CSS but prevent minification: 114 115 ```js 116 import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/postcss' 117 118 export default { 119 plugins: [ 120 tailwindcss({ 121 // Enables Lightning CSS but disables minification 122 optimize: { minify: false }, 123 }), 124 ], 125 } 126 ``` 127
